r. A. Hutchinson, Fighting Dick"
as he is known among his friends,
returned to the city last night aft
err so mile ride through the coun-
try districts in which time he
spoke to over 200 voters.


He feels confident of the out
come and says he has every rea-
son to feel confident that he will
be selected by the people of the
Fourth senatorial district to be the
candidate on the republican ticket
in the November election.


Mr.. Hutchinson's attention was
called to campaign document is
sued by his opponent, Senator w.
d. Scott, in which it is stated that
Mr., Hutchinson voted against the
eight hour bill in the legislative
session of 1891. This Mr.. Hutchin-
son states is not true, although
the record does show that he voted
against the bill. He says that he
voted against recommitting the
bill, but did vote for it's passage.


In this statement he Is support
ed by a number of local trade
unionists, notably c. B. Nash, now
superintendent of the Shaw Borden
Co. printing department, who was
at that time a delegate to the
trades council in this city.


The records of the local trades
council show that Mr.. Hutchinson
was endorsed by that body for his
services to labor and the people in
general during theNsession OF 1891.
He was also endorsed by the In
dustrial legion for the office OF
United States senator in recogni-
ton of his public services and SYM
pathy with the masses. Mr., Hutch
inson further stated that he is in
every respect a self made man and
was for years a miner and mem
Der of the union al Newcastle,
King county, and served as secre-
tary of the union for some time.
Under these circumstances" he
says, ''it is unlikely that | would
vote against any law having for its
object the betterment of the work
ing class"
